TextBlock.Text: Kremlin calls pact a strategic trap; NATO officials alarmed

StackPanel.TextBlock:
MOSCOW — In a dramatic turn, the Soviet Union announced today that it is suspending participation in the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces (INF) Treaty, citing what it calls “unrelenting Western duplicity” and “NATO’s encirclement strategy.”
General Secretary Mikhail Gorbachev, in a televised address to the Supreme Soviet, accused the United States of violating the treaty’s “spirit and letter” through continued military build-up in the Arctic and covert missile deployments to NATO-aligned states. “We will not disarm while NATO sharpens the knife,” Gorbachev declared.
The INF Treaty, signed just three months ago, had mandated the destruction of thousands of nuclear-capable missiles in Europe and marked a historic thaw in superpower relations. Today’s withdrawal sends shockwaves through diplomatic circles and threatens to unravel years of arms control efforts. (continued on page 2)

TextBlock.Text: Soviet troops parade advanced intermediate-range ballistic missiles through Red Square during Victory Day celebrations in Moscow. Showcasing the power of the Strategic Rocket Forces, these missiles are capable of striking virtually all major NATO cities within minutes. The now-suspended INF Treaty had aimed to eliminate such weapons from both sides' arsenals, marking a critical step toward de-escalation and lasting arms control. (AP Wire Photo)
SOVIET BOMBERS PUSH LIMITS IN NORTH SEA AIRSPACE

StackPanel.TextBlock:
OSLO – Two Soviet Tu-95 “Bear” bombers flew within 25 miles of the Norwegian coast Friday, prompting NATO fighter intercepts for the third time this month. British and Norwegian officials confirmed that RAF Tornados and RNoAF F-16s were scrambled to shadow the bombers as they skirted the air defense identification zones (ADIZ) of both nations.
Military analysts say the overflights are part of a growing pattern of Soviet probing missions intended to test NATO reaction times and gather electronic intelligence. “They’re getting bolder,” said one Norwegian defense source. “This wasn’t a routine patrol. It was a message.”
No hostile action occurred, but Norwegian Foreign Minister Thorvald Stoltenberg called the flight “provocative” and warned Moscow against further brinkmanship. The Soviet Foreign Ministry responded by accusing NATO of “hysteria” and “militarizing the Arctic frontier.”

TextBlock.Text: A Soviet Tu-95 “Bear” bomber cruises above the Barents Sea during a long-range patrol. This aircraft type is frequently used by Soviet Naval Aviation for maritime surveillance, electronic intelligence gathering, and probing NATO air defenses. With its distinctive turboprop engines and long endurance, the Bear has become a familiar—and provocative—presence near the airspace of Norway, the United Kingdom, and Iceland. U.S. officials have condemned the overflights as deliberate escalations intended to test Allied response times and resolve. (DoD File Photo)
